front: Artist: Co-founded the Photo-Secession movement and Gallery 291 with Alfred Stieglitz
Artist: Curated the 1955 MoMA exhibition The Family of Man, selecting 503 photos from 68 countries
Photograph: Sold for $2.9 million in 2006, then the world's most expensive photograph ever; taken at Mamaroneck, NY
Photograph: Used gum bichromate to manually layer color onto the negative, producing a Pictorialist effect
Photograph: Night photograph of a New York skyscraper behind tree branches; used gum bichromate to produce a blue background
Artist: Pioneer of fashion photography; chief photographer for Vogue and Vanity Fair in the 1920s–30s
work: Fashion Photography / Vogue and Vanity Fair

front: Artist: 1911 photographs of Paul Poiret's gowns for Art et Decoration were considered the first modern fashion photographs
work: Fashion Photography / Vogue and Vanity Fair

front: Artist: Photographed Gloria Swanson behind a piece of black lace, likening her to a leopardess
work: Fashion Photography / Vogue and Vanity Fair

front: Artist: Won an Oscar for directing this WWII documentary, in which narrator Robert Taylor says all servicemen shown have been killed
Photograph: Shows Rodin's monument to Balzac; photographed by Rodin's friend
work: Rodin Photographs / Towards the Light, Midnight

front: Artist: Most frequently featured artist in Camera Work; also designed its covers
Artist: Native Luxembourger; avid horticulturist who bred new varieties of delphinium
Artist: At age 14, Stephen Shore cold-called this photographer and sold him three photos to launch Shore's career
